topic: A person finds out their article was published under someone else's name
raw_output:
[story 1]

The email landed in my inbox like a physical blow. A polite, yet utterly devastating notification confirming that the piece I poured my soul into—the research, the late nights, the sheer intellectual wrestling match—was officially credited to 'Alistair Vance.' My fingers hovered over the keyboard, trembling slightly. I reread the attribution, the carefully chosen byline, and a cold, heavy sensation settled in my chest, tightening my diaphragm until drawing a full breath felt like an effort. I looked around my small, cluttered office, the fluorescent lights buzzing with an irritating, relentless hum. Every corner seemed to press in, the walls seeming to lean inward, suffocating the air. I started pacing the worn rug, my footsteps echoing hollowly in the silence. Who was Alistair Vance? How could someone else have stolen this? I wanted to scream, but the sound caught in my throat, leaving only a dry, rasping sound.

[story 2]

I stared at the screen, the words shimmering mockingly on the page. The realization hit me not as a sudden shock, but as a slow, creeping paralysis. It felt as if the floor had dissolved beneath my feet, leaving me suspended in a murky, shallow water. My hands clenched into fists so tight my nails dug crescents into my palms. I slowly pushed back from the desk, the chair scraping loudly against the wooden floor, a jarring noise in the quiet apartment. I walked to the window, pulling the curtain aside just enough to glimpse the indifferent city below. The sheer volume of possibility—the endless paths I had planned, the certainty of my own contribution—suddenly evaporated. A deep, nauseating wave washed over me, making my vision swim. I sank onto the sofa, burying my face in my hands, the muffled sound of my own ragged breathing the only evidence that I was still anchored to reality.

[story 3]

The printed copy lay on the table, stark white paper mocking me with its legitimacy. I picked it up, the edges crisp beneath my fingertips, and the ink of the borrowed name felt like a foreign brand burned onto my own identity. A strange, hollow ache resonated deep within my sternum, a pressure that made my shoulders hunch forward. I tried to formulate a response, to demand an explanation, but the words refused to form, snagged somewhere between my lungs and my tongue. Every muscle in my body felt coiled, ready for a fight that I had no energy to wage. I began methodically stacking the papers, the movements slow, deliberate, each placement an act of forced control over a situation that had utterly stripped me bare. The silence of the room amplified the frantic drumming inside my own ears, a relentless rhythm signaling that I was utterly out of control.
